name: Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) – Refresh v2.0
description: Board-approved Risk Appetite Statement covering capital adequacy, liquidity, credit, market, operational, reputational/ESG, and strategic risk with quantitative limits and qualitative statements.
content:
---
title: Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) – Refresh
version: 2.0
effective_date: 2025-Q2
status: Final (Board Approved)
---

## 1. Purpose & Scope

This Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) defines the aggregate level and types of risk the Institution is willing to accept in pursuit of its strategic objectives. The RAS aligns with the ICAAP (Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process) and applies to all business lines, legal entities, and subsidiaries.

## 2. Risk Appetite Principles

| Principle | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| P1 – Board Ownership | The Board of Directors approves the RAS annually and ensures it is embedded in strategic decision-making. |
| P2 – Quantitative & Qualitative | Risk appetite is expressed through both quantitative limits and qualitative statements reflecting risk culture. |
| P3 – Forward-Looking | The RAS incorporates stress testing and scenario analysis to anticipate adverse conditions. |
| P4 – Proportionality | Limits are proportionate to the Institution’s size, complexity, and systemic importance. |
| P5 – Integrated | The RAS is cascaded throughout the organisation via a hierarchical limit system. |
| P6 – Escalation & Remediation | Breaches are promptly identified, escalated, and remediated per the Breach Escalation Protocol (Section 5). |

## 3. Quantitative Risk Appetite Statements

### 3.1 Capital Adequacy
| Metric | Appetite Statement | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| CET1 Ratio | ≥ 10.5% | 10.5% | 11.5% |
| Tier 1 Capital Ratio | ≥ 12.0% | 12.0% | 13.0% |
| Total Capital Ratio (CAR) | ≥ 14.0% | 14.0% | 15.5% |
| Leverage Ratio | ≥ 4.5% | 4.5% | 5.0% |

### 3.2 Liquidity & Funding
| Metric | Appetite Statement | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| LCR (Liquidity Coverage Ratio) | ≥ 110% | 110% | 120% |
| NSFR (Net Stable Funding Ratio) | ≥ 105% | 105% | 110% |
| Loan-to-Deposit Ratio | 80%–100% | 100% | 95% |
| Wholesale Funding Dependency | ≤ 35% of total funding | 35% | 30% |

### 3.3 Credit Risk
| Metric | Appetite Statement | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| NPL Ratio (Non-Performing Loans) | ≤ 3.5% | 3.5% | 2.5% |
| Expected Loss (EL) to Gross Exposure | ≤ 1.0% | 1.0% | 0.8% |
| Single Name Concentration (largest obligor) | ≤ 10% of Tier 1 Capital | 10% | 8% |
| Top 20 Obligor Concentration | ≤ 40% of Tier 1 Capital | 40% | 35% |
| Sectoral Exposure (any single sector) | ≤ 25% of total loan book | 25% | 20% |

### 3.4 Market Risk
| Metric | Appetite Statement | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| VaR (99%, 1-day) | ≤ £5 million | £5M | £4M |
| Stressed VaR (99%, 1-day) | ≤ £8 million | £8M | £6M |
| PV01 (per bp) | ≤ £2.5 million | £2.5M | £2.0M |
| Net Open FX Position | ≤ 5% of Tier 1 Capital | 5% | 4% |

### 3.5 Operational Risk
| Metric | Appetite Statement | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| Operational Risk Loss (annual) | ≤ £20 million | £20M | £15M |
| Critical Process Downtime (per event) | ≤ 4 hours | 4 hrs | 2 hrs |
| IT System Availability (core banking) | ≥ 99.95% | 99.95% | 99.98% |

### 3.6 Reputational & ESG Risk
- The Institution will not engage in activities that could cause material reputational harm or violate ESG commitments.
- No financing for coal-fired power plants or thermal coal mining (new projects).
- Net-zero commitment by 2050 with intermediate targets reviewed annually.
- All third-party relationships to be subject to enhanced due diligence for ESG and reputational risk.

### 3.7 Strategic & Business Risk
- Maximum deviation from budgeted annual net profit: ≤ 15%.
- Minimum RoE (Return on Equity): ≥ 8%.
- New business lines or products must have Board-approved RAS integration before launch.

## 4. Risk Capacity vs. Appetite

Risk Capacity (the maximum risk the Institution can bear before breaching regulatory or solvency constraints) exceeds Risk Appetite by a minimum buffer of 15% at the aggregate level.

- **Risk Capacity**: Total Capital Ratio of 16%+ (under stress)
- **Risk Appetite Upper Bound**: Total Capital Ratio of 14.0%
- **Buffer**: 2.0% (~12.5% of appetite level)

## 5. Review & Governance

- **Owner**: Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
- **Review Frequency**: Annually (or upon material change in strategy, risk profile, or regulation)
- **Approval**: Board of Directors (Risk Committee recommendation)
- **Exceptions**: Any exception to RAS must be documented, justified, and approved by the Board Risk Committee.

## 6. Attestation

This RAS has been reviewed by:
- CRO: [Sign] ___ Date: ___ | CEO: [Sign] ___ Date: ___ | Board Chair: [Sign] ___ Date: ___

name: Hierarchical Limit System (HLS) – v2.0
description: Three-tier cascading limit framework from Board-level (Tier 1) through business lines (Tier 2) to desks/portfolios (Tier 3), with limit allocation methodology and classification.
content:
---
title: Hierarchical Limit System (HLS)
version: 2.0
effective_date: 2025-Q2
status: Operational
---

## 1. Framework Overview

The Hierarchical Limit System cascades the Institution’s Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) into measurable, actionable limits at three tiers. Each tier is more granular, enabling risk aggregation and oversight from the Board to the individual trading desk / business unit.

```
Tier 1 (Board / Institution Level)
       ▼
Tier 2 (Business Line / Legal Entity Level)
       ▼
Tier 3 (Desk / Portfolio / Product Level)
```

## 2. Tier 1 – Institution-Level Limits (Board / Executive Committee)

Tier 1 limits correspond directly to the RAS quantitative metrics. These are the “hard” boundaries of risk appetite.

| Risk Type | Limit Metric | Hard Limit | Soft Trigger | Reporting Frequency |
|-----------|-------------|------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Capital | CET1 Ratio | ≥ 10.5% | ≤ 11.5% | Daily |
| Capital | Total CAR | ≥ 14.0% | ≤ 15.5% | Daily |
| Liquidity | LCR | ≥ 110% | ≤ 120% | Daily |
| Liquidity | NSFR | ≥ 105% | ≤ 110% | Monthly |
| Credit | NPL Ratio | ≤ 3.5% | ≥ 2.5% | Monthly |
| Credit | Single Name Conc. | ≤ 10% of T1 Cap | ≥ 8% | Weekly |
| Market | VaR (99%, 1d) | ≤ £5M | ≥ £4M | Daily |
| Market | Stressed VaR | ≤ £8M | ≥ £6M | Daily |
| Operational | Annual Loss | ≤ £20M | ≥ £15M | Quarterly |

## 3. Tier 2 – Business Line / Legal Entity Limits

Each business line receives an allocation of Tier 1 limits, proportionally allocated based on RWA contribution, earnings, and strategic relevance.

### 3.1 Retail Banking
| Metric | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------|--------|
| CET1 Allocation | ≥ 11.0% | 12.0% |
| NPL Ratio | ≤ 4.0% | 3.0% |
| LCR | ≥ 115% | 125% |
| Loan Growth Cap (YoY) | ≤ 8% | 6% |
| Household Sector Limit | ≤ 60% of retail portfolio | 55% |

### 3.2 Corporate & Investment Banking
| Metric | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------|--------|
| CET1 Allocation | ≥ 10.5% | 11.5% |
| VaR (99%, 1d) | ≤ £2.5M | £2.0M |
| Single Name Limit | ≤ 10% of T1 Cap | 8% |
| Counterparty Credit Risk (CCR) | ≤ £500M | ¥400M |
| Leveraged Finance Exposure | ≤ 15% of lending book | 12% |

### 3.3 Asset Management / Wealth
| Metric | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------|--------|
| VaR (99%, 1d) | ≤ £1.0M | £0.8M |
| AUM Concentration (top 10 clients) | ≤ 30% | 25% |
| Alternative Investments Allocation | ≤ 20% of AUM | 15% |

### 3.4 Treasury / ALM
| Metric | Limit | Trigger |
|--------|-------|--------|
| LCR | ≥ 120% | 130% |
| NSFR | ≥ 105% | 110% |
| Net Open FX Position | ≤ 5% of T1 Cap | 4% |
| Interest Rate Risk in Banking Book (IRRBB) | ≤ 15% of T1 Cap | 12% |
| Intraday Liquidity Cap | ≤ £2B peak | £1.5B |

### 3.5 Subsidiaries (per jurisdiction)
Each regulated subsidiary maintains a standalone limit set aligned with local regulatory minima and Group RAS, subject to the more restrictive of the two.

## 4. Tier 3 – Desk / Portfolio / Product Limits

### 4.1 Trading Desk Limits
| Desk | VaR Limit (99%, 1d) | Stop-Loss (monthly) | DV01 | Max Position |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|------|--------------|
| Fixed Income | £1.0M | £3.0M | £500K | £500M |
| FX | £500K | £1.5M | N/A | £250M |
| Equities | £750K | £2.0M | N/A | £300M |
| Credit Trading | £600K | £1.8M | ¥350K | £200M |
| Commodities | ¥400K | £1.2M | N/A | £150M |

### 4.2 Credit Portfolio Limits
| Sub-Portfolio | Maximum Exposure | Max NPL | Sector Cap |
|--------------|-----------------|---------|------------|
| Mortgages | £5B | 2.5% | Residential Only |
| SME Loans | £2B | 4.0% | Diversified |
| Large Corporates | £3B | 3.0% | ≤ 20% per sector |
| Project Finance | £500M | 2.0% | Infrastructure |

### 4.3 Operational Risk Limits (per process / system)
| Process / System | Max Single Loss | Max Annual Loss | Max Downtime |
|-----------------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| Core Banking System | £5M | £10M | 4 hrs |
| Digital Channels | £2M | £5M | 2 hrs |
| Payment Systems | £3M | £7M | 1 hr |
| Data Centers | £4M | £8M | 4 hrs |

## 5. Limit Allocation Methodology

Limits are allocated from Tier 1 downward using:

1. **Top-Down Calibration**: Board sets Tier 1 limits based on RAS and ICAAP.
2. **Proportional Allocation**: Tier 2 limits are allocated based on RWA share, revenue contribution, historical volatility, and stress test results.
3. **Bottom-Up Validation**: Business lines submit proposed Tier 3 limits; aggregated values must not exceed Tier 2 envelopes.
4. **Annual Re-Calibration**: Full review of allocation methodology annually; mid-year adjustments allowed for material changes.

## 6. Limit Breach Classification

| Breach Severity | Tier 1 Trigger? | Tier 2 Trigger? | Tier 3 Trigger? |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Green** | No breach | No breach | Within trigger |
| **Amber** | Within trigger | Within trigger | Limit breached |
| **Orange** | Within trigger | Limit breached | Limit breached |
| **Red** | Limit breached | Limit breached | Limit breached |

## 7. Governance & Maintenance

- **Owner**: CRO (Tier 1), Head of Risk (Tier 2), Desk Heads (Tier 3)
- **Review Cycle**: Annually with quarterly health checks
- **System of Record**: RiskLimitManager v4.2 (central limit monitoring system)
- **Override Authority**: Only the Board Risk Committee can override/amend Tier 1 limits (temporary, with documented rationale).

name: Breach Escalation Protocol (BEP) – v2.0
description: Tiered breach escalation protocol with severity classification, escalation matrix, reporting templates, remediation actions, risk acceptance rules, and governance for limit breaches.
content:
---
title: Breach Escalation Protocol (BEP)
version: 2.0
effective_date: 2025-Q2
status: Operational
---

## 1. Purpose

This Breach Escalation Protocol defines the processes, responsibilities, timelines, and remediation requirements when risk appetite limits or hierarchical limits are breached. The protocol ensures timely identification, escalation, and corrective action to maintain the Institution’s risk profile within approved boundaries.

## 2. Breach Severity Classification

| Level | Severity | Colour Code | Definition |
|-------|----------|-------------|------------|
| 0 | No Breach | 🟢 Green | All limits satisfied; values within triggers |
| 1 | Mild Breach | 🟡 Amber | Tier 3 limit breached OR Tier 2 trigger crossed |
| 2 | Moderate Breach | 🟠 Orange | Tier 2 limit breached (any metric) |
| 3 | Critical Breach | 🔴 Red | Tier 1 limit breached (RAS violation) |

## 3. Detection & Monitoring

| Mechanism | Frequency | Owner |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Automated limit monitoring system (RiskLimitManager) | Real-time / Daily | Market Risk & Credit Risk teams |
| Manual attestation by business line heads | Weekly | Business Lines |
| Independent risk reviews | Monthly | Second Line of Defence (Risk) |
| Internal Audit spot checks | Quarterly | Third Line of Defence (Audit) |
| Stress testing / reverse stress testing | Quarterly | Enterprise Risk Management |

## 4. Escalation Matrix

### Level 1 – Amber (Mild Breach)
| Step | Action | Responsible | Timeline |
|------|--------|-------------|----------|
| 1.1 | Automated alert generated | Risk System | Immediate |
| 1.2 | Desk Head notified; immediate action to return within limit | Desk Head | Within 1 hour |
| 1.3 | Root cause analysis initiated | Business Line Risk | Within 24 hours |
| 1.4 | Remediation plan submitted to Business Line Head | Desk Head | Within 48 hours |
| 1.5 | Remediation completed and verified | Business Line Risk | Within 5 business days |

### Level 2 – Orange (Moderate Breach)
| Step | Action | Responsible | Timeline |
|------|--------|-------------|----------|
| 2.1 | Automated alert + escalation to Tier 2 | Risk System | Immediate |
| 2.2 | Business Line Head notified | CRO Office | Within 30 mins |
| 2.3 | Immediate risk-reducing actions (hedging, unwinding) | Desk Head + Risk | Within 2 hours |
| 2.4 | Formal breach report prepared | Business Line Risk | Within 24 hours |
| 2.5 | Report submitted to CRO and Business Line Head | Risk | Within 24 hours |
| 2.6 | Remediation plan approved by CRO | CRO | Within 48 hours |
| 2.7 | Remediation completed | Business Line | Within 10 business days |
| 2.8 | Post-mortem review with Risk Committee | CRO | Within 30 days |

### Level 3 – Red (Critical Breach / RAS Violation)
| Step | Action | Responsible | Timeline |
|------|--------|-------------|----------|
| 3.1 | System-generated critical alert | Risk System | Immediate |
| 3.2 | CEO + CRO + Board Risk Committee Chair notified | CRO Office | Within 15 mins |
| 3.3 | Emergency risk reduction measures activated | CRO + Business Line | Within 1 hour |
| 3.4 | Full Board of Directors notified | CEO | Within 4 hours |
| 3.5 | Preliminary breach report (quant impact, root cause) | CRO | Within 24 hours |
| 3.6 | Comprehensive breach report + remediation plan | CRO | Within 48 hours |
| 3.7 | Remediation actions executed | Business Line + Risk | Within 15 business days |
| 3.8 | Regulatory notification (if applicable) | CEO + CRO | As per regulatory requirement (typically 24–72 hrs) |
| 3.9 | Full post-mortem to Board Risk Committee | CRO | Within 45 days |
| 3.10 | RAS / Limit framework review triggered | Board Risk Committee | Within 60 days |

## 5. Breach Reporting Template

Every formal breach report (Level 2+) must include:

```
BREACH REPORT
─────────────────────────────────────
1. Breach ID: [auto-generated]
2. Date/Time: [timestamp]
3. Severity Level: [Amber / Orange / Red]
4. Risk Type: [Credit / Market / Liquidity / Operational / etc.]
5. Limit Metric: [e.g., CET1 Ratio]
6. Limit Value: [e.g., 10.5%]
7. Actual Value: [e.g., 10.2%]
8. Duration of Breach: [e.g., 3 days]
9. Root Cause: [narrative]
10. Immediate Actions Taken: [list]
11. Remediation Plan: [steps, owners, timelines]
12. Residual Risk After Remediation: [assessment]
13. Lessons Learned: [narrative]
14. Attachments: [supporting data]
─────────────────────────────────────
```

## 6. Remediation Actions by Breach Type

| Breach Type | Typical Remediation Actions |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| Capital Ratio breach | Capital raising, RWA reduction, dividend suspension, portfolio optimisation |
| Liquidity breach | Activate contingency funding plan, draw committed lines, liquidate HQLA, reduce lending |
| Credit limit breach | Reduce exposure, obtain additional collateral, hedge with CDS, loan sale / securitisation |
| Market risk (VaR) breach | Reduce positions, add hedging, impose trading restrictions, review risk models |
| Operational loss breach | Process redesign, system patches, compensating controls, insurance recovery |
| Concentration breach | Portfolio diversification, syndication, secondary market sales |

## 7. Breach Register

All breaches (Level 1+) are recorded in a central Breach Register maintained by the CRO Office.

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Breach ID | Unique identifier |
| Date | When breach occurred |
| Severity | Amber / Orange / Red |
| Limit Type | Capital / Liquidity / Credit / Market / Operational |
| Duration | Time from detection to resolution |
| Root Cause | Categorised (model error, market move, operational error, etc.) |
| Remediation Status | Open / In Progress / Resolved / Accepted |
| Accepted Risk? | If risk accepted, Board approval reference |

## 8. Risk Acceptance & Exceptions

- **Level 1 (Amber)**: Business Line Head may accept the risk for up to 5 business days; must document rationale.
- **Level 2 (Orange)**: CRO may accept the risk for up to 15 business days; requires documented mitigation.
- **Level 3 (Red)**: Only the Board Risk Committee can accept a RAS breach; must be time-limited (≤ 30 days) and reported to regulators if required.
- No retroactive acceptance permitted.

## 9. System Integration

- **Primary System**: RiskLimitManager v4.2 (real-time monitoring, auto-escalation, alerts via email/SMS/Teams)
- **Backup System**: Manual escalation via Risk Control team (within 30 mins if system unavailable)
- **Reporting Dashboards**: Real-time risk dashboard for CRO/CEO; weekly summary for Board Risk Committee

## 10. Testing & Maintenance

| Activity | Frequency | Owner |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| Tabletop breach escalation exercises | Quarterly | CRO Office |
| Full simulation of Level 3 breach | Annually | CRO + Internal Audit |
| Review and update of BEP | Annually | CRO Office + Board Risk Committee |
| System failover testing | Semi-annually | IT / Risk Technology |

## 11. Consequences & Accountability

- **First offence (any level)**: Root cause analysis, remediation, verbal warning to Desk Head
- **Repeat offence (same limit within 12 months)**: Written warning + enhanced monitoring + mandatory retraining
- **Wilful breach or concealment**: Disciplinary action up to and including dismissal; regulatory notification as required
- **Systemic breach pattern**: Organisation-wide limit review, potential restructuring of affected business line

## 12. Appendices

- **Appendix A**: Contact tree (Board, CRO, CEO, Risk, Legal, Compliance, Regulatory Reporting)
- **Appendix B**: Limit breach form (standard template for all levels)
- **Appendix C**: System escalation configuration parameters (RiskLimitManager thresholds)
- **Appendix D**: Regulatory notification checklist (by jurisdiction)

name: icaap-architecture-doc.md
description: Comprehensive ICAAP architecture document covering system architecture, governance integration, planning cycle alignment, data architecture, stress testing framework, and implementation roadmap.
content:
# ICAAP Architecture Document

## Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process – Architecture and Integration with Planning and Governance

---

## Document Control

| Metadata | Value |
|---|---|
| **Document Title** | ICAAP Architecture and Integration Framework |
| **Document ID** | ICAAP-ARCH-001 |
| **Version** | 1.0 |
| **Status** | Draft |
| **Owner** | Chief Risk Officer (CRO) |
| **Approving Body** | Board Risk Committee |
| **Review Cycle** | Annual |
| **Regulatory Framework** | Basel II/III, CRD IV/CRR, Local Regulator Guidelines |

---

## 1. Executive Summary

The Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) is a cornerstone of the Pillar 2 framework under Basel regulations. This document defines the architectural blueprint for the ICAAP framework, detailing its structural components, data flows, systems integration points, and its seamless integration with the institution's business planning and governance processes.

The architecture described herein ensures that capital adequacy is not viewed in isolation but as an integral part of the strategic planning cycle, risk appetite framework, and overall corporate governance structure.

---

## 2. Purpose and Scope

### 2.1 Purpose
- Define the architectural components of the ICAAP framework
- Establish clear integration points with strategic planning and budgeting
- Document governance structures supporting ICAAP
- Provide a reference architecture for implementation and audit

### 2.2 Scope
- All material risk types (credit, market, operational, liquidity, concentration, interest rate risk in the banking book, business/strategic risk)
- All legal entities and business units within the consolidation scope
- All capital planning, stress testing, and risk measurement systems
- Integration with the Internal Liquidity Adequacy Assessment Process (ILAAP)

---

## 3. ICAAP Architectural Overview

### 3.1 High-Level Architecture

The ICAAP architecture is structured across three interconnected layers:

```
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    GOVERNANCE LAYER                               |
|  Board | Board Risk Committee | CRO | ALCO | Risk Management     |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
                              |
                              v
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    ORCHESTRATION LAYER                             |
|  ICAAP Policy | Risk Appetite Framework | Capital Strategy        |
|  Stress Testing Framework | Recovery Planning | Capital Buffer Mgt |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
                              |
                              v
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    DATA & SYSTEMS LAYER                            |
|  Risk Data Warehouse | ALM System | Credit Risk System            |
|  Market Risk System | Operational Risk System | Fin. Planning     |
|  Stress Testing Engine | Reporting & Dashboard Platform           |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

### 3.2 Architectural Principles

1. **Comprehensive Risk Coverage** – All material risks must be identified, measured, and aggregated.
2. **Forward-Looking Perspective** – Architecture must support both current and projected risk profiles.
3. **Proportionality** – Sophistication of architecture must be proportional to size, complexity, and risk profile.
4. **Independence** – Risk measurement and validation functions must be independent from risk-taking functions.
5. **Integration** – ICAAP must be integrated into management processes and decision-making.
6. **Auditability** – All processes, models, and assumptions must be fully documented and auditable.
7. **Data Integrity** – Data lineage, quality controls, and reconciliation must be embedded.

---

## 4. Core Architectural Components

### 4.1 Risk Identification and Materiality Assessment

| Component | Description | System/Process Integration |
|---|---|---|
| Risk Inventory | Comprehensive register of all identifiable risks | Integrated with Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) system |
| Materiality Thresholds | Quantitative and qualitative criteria for risk materiality | Defined in Risk Appetite Framework (RAF) |
| Emerging Risk Scan | Forward-looking identification of new and evolving risks | Input from strategy planning, external environment analysis |
| Risk Categorization | Classification into Pillar 1 and Pillar 2 risks | Mapped to regulatory reporting taxonomies |

### 4.2 Risk Measurement and Quantification

| Component | Methodology | Integration Point |
|---|---|---|
| Credit Risk | AIRB/SA, Expected Loss models, Credit VaR | Credit risk system, loan portfolio data |
| Market Risk | VaR, Stressed VaR, RVaR, Sensitivities | Market risk system, trading book data |
| Operational Risk | AMA, BIA, TSA, Scenario Analysis, LDA | OR system, loss data collection |
| IRRBB | Earnings at Risk, Economic Value of Equity | ALM system, behavioral models |
| Concentration Risk | Herfindahl-Hirschman Index, Granularity Adjustment | Portfolio analytics |
| Liquidity Risk | LCR, NSFR, Stress scenarios | ALM / Treasury system |
| Business/Strategic Risk | Earnings volatility, Capital consumption projections | Financial planning system |

### 4.3 Capital Calculation Engine

```
+-------------------+     +-------------------+     +-------------------+
| Risk-Weighted     | --> | Capital Charge    | --> | Available         |
| Assets (RWA)      |     | Calculation       |     | Capital Resources |
+-------------------+     +-------------------+     +-------------------+
         |                        |                          |
         v                        v                          v
+-------------------+     +-------------------+     +-------------------+
| Pillar 1 RWA      |     | Pillar 2 Add-ons  |     | CET1, AT1, T2     |
| - Credit RWA      |     | - Concentration   |     | - Capital ratios  |
| - Market RWA      |     | - IRRBB           |     | - Leverage ratio  |
| - Operational RWA |     | - Other risks     |     | - MREL/MDA        |
+-------------------+     +-------------------+     +-------------------+
```

### 4.4 Stress Testing Framework

| Stress Type | Time Horizon | Coverage | Scenario Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline | 3-5 years | All material risks | Central forecast (business plan) |
| Adverse | 3-5 years | All material risks | Regulator-defined + Internal scenarios |
| Severely Adverse | 3-5 years | All material risks | Tail risk scenarios |
| Reverse Stress Test | Variable | Identify point of non-viability | Firm-specific vulnerabilities |
| Sensitivity Analysis | 1-3 years | Single risk factor shocks | Risk-specific |

The stress testing architecture must support:
- **Bottom-up** (business unit level) and **top-down** (enterprise level) approaches
- **Static** and **dynamic** balance sheet assumptions
- **Scenario generation** with macro-financial linkages
- **Capital impact aggregation** across risk types
- **Management actions** simulation capability

### 4.5 Capital Planning and Target Setting

| Architectural Element | Description |
|---|---|
| Capital Targets | Board-approved target capital ratios (Pillar 2 Guidance + buffers) |
| Capital Buffers | CCB, CCyB, G-SII/O-SII, Systemic risk buffer, P2G |
| Capital Triggers | Hard limits, early warning triggers, escalation thresholds |
| Capital Budgeting | Allocation of capital to business lines based on RAROC/RoRAC |
| Capital Contingency Plan | Actions to restore capital under stress (capital conservation, raising, RWA reduction) |

---

## 5. Integration with Business Planning

### 5.1 Planning Cycle Alignment

The ICAAP is fully integrated into the annual business planning and budgeting cycle:

```
Q1                           Q2                           Q3                           Q4
|                            |                            |                            |
| Strategic Review           | Business Plan              | Budget Finalization        | Plan Approval             |
| Risk Appetite Review       | Development                | ICAAP Update                | Board Approval            |
| Macroeconomic Outlook      | Capital Plan               | Stress Test Results         | Capital Ratios Published  |
| Risk Identification        | Stress Test Execution      | Management Actions          | ICAAP Report Submission   |
|                            | Scenario Calibration       | Capital Targets Refinement  | Regulator Engagement      |
v                            v                            v                            v
```

### 5.2 Key Integration Points

| Integration Point | Business Planning Component | ICAAP Component | Data/Information Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Strategic Direction** | Long-term strategy, growth targets | Risk appetite, capital adequacy targets | Strategic plan informs risk capacity limits |
| **2. Financial Projections** | P&L, Balance sheet, Budgets | Capital projections, RWA forecasts | Financial plan feeds capital model inputs |
| **3. Risk Assessment** | Risk identification in new initiatives | Risk quantification, capital requirements | New business risks assessed for capital impact |
| **4. Scenario Planning** | Business scenarios, contingencies | Stress testing, capital adequacy under stress | Consistent scenarios across planning and ICAAP |
| **5. Resource Allocation** | Budget allocation, investment decisions | ICAAP capital allocation, risk-adjusted returns | Capital constraints influence resource allocation |
| **6. Performance Management** | KPI targets, balanced scorecard | Risk-adjusted performance (RAROC), capital efficiency | Capital usage linked to performance metrics |

### 5.3 Data Flow Between Planning and ICAAP

```
+------------------------+          +------------------------+
|   FINANCIAL PLANNING   |          |     ICAAP SYSTEM        |
|   SYSTEM               |          |                        |
|                        |          |                        |
| - P&L Forecasts -------+--------->| - Capital Generation   |
| - Balance Sheet ------+---------->| - RWA Projections      |
| - Budget Allocations --+--------->| - Capital Allocation   |
| - Strategic Initiatives |         | - Stress Impact        |
+------------------------+          +------------------------+
         ^                                 |
         |                                 |
         +-------- Constraints/Limits -----+
         |   (Capital targets, buffers,    |
         |    risk limits, concentration   |
         |    thresholds)                  |
         +---------------------------------+
```

### 5.4 Feedback Loops

| Loop | Description | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Strategic Feedback | Capital constraints feed back into strategic planning | Annual |
| Tactical Feedback | Capital consumption vs. targets drives tactical adjustments | Quarterly |
| Operational Feedback | Limit breaches and early warning triggers drive immediate action | Ad-hoc / Daily |
| Validation Feedback | Model validation and back-testing results inform planning assumptions | Annual |

---

## 6. Integration with Governance

### 6.1 Governance Structure Overview

```
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                          BOARD                               |
|  - Approves ICAAP policy, risk appetite, capital targets    |
|  - Oversees capital adequacy and risk profile               |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|              BOARD RISK COMMITTEE (BRC)                      |
|  - Reviews ICAAP methodology and results                    |
|  - Monitors risk profile vs. appetite                       |
|  - Approves stress testing scenarios                        |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|            CHIEF RISK OFFICER (CRO)                          |
|  - Owns ICAAP framework                                      |
|  - Reports to BRC and CEO                                   |
|  - Independent risk oversight                                |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
         |                       |                       |
         v                       v                       v
+----------------+   +----------------+   +----------------+
|  RISK          |   |  ALCO / TREASURY |   |  CREDIT       |
|  COMMITTEE     |   |  - Liquidity     |   |  COMMITTEE    |
|  - Operational |   |  - IRRBB         |   |  - Credit risk|
|  - Market risk |   |  - Funding       |   |  - Portfolio  |
+----------------+   +----------------+   +----------------+
```

### 6.2 Three Lines of Defense Model

| Line of Defense | Role in ICAAP | Responsibilities |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Line** (Business Units) | Risk ownership, data provision | Implement controls, provide risk data, execute management actions, adhere to limits |
| **2nd Line** (Risk Management, Compliance) | ICAAP ownership, framework design | Develop ICAAP methodology, measure/report risks, challenge 1st line, ensure regulatory compliance |
| **3rd Line** (Internal Audit) | Independent assurance | Audit ICAAP processes, validate model governance, assess framework adequacy, report to BRC |

### 6.3 Key Governance Forums and Their ICAAP Responsibilities

| Forum | Frequency | ICAAP Responsibilities |
|---|---|---|
| **Board** | Quarterly / Ad-hoc | Approve ICAAP policy, risk appetite, capital targets, ICAAP report |
| **Board Risk Committee** | Quarterly | Review ICAAP methodology, stress test results, risk profile, concentration limits |
| **Board Audit Committee** | Quarterly | Oversee ICAAP audit findings, model validation outcomes |
| **Executive Committee** | Monthly | Review capital position, approve management actions, monitor limit compliance |
| **Risk Committee (Manco)** | Monthly | Review risk metrics, stress test results, emerging risks, capital projections |
| **ALCO** | Monthly | Review IRRBB, liquidity risk, funding plan, interest rate risk capital |
| **Credit Committee** | Weekly/Monthly | Review credit portfolio quality, impairment, large exposures |
| **Model Governance Committee** | Quarterly | Approve risk models, review validation results, approve model changes |
| **Capital Committee** | Quarterly | Capital planning, allocation, optimization, issuance recommendations |

### 6.4 ICAAP Governance Processes

#### 6.4.1 Policy Framework

The ICAAP policy framework consists of:
1. **ICAAP Policy** – High-level framework, roles, responsibilities, principles
2. **Risk Appetite Statement (RAS)** – Quantitative and qualitative risk tolerances
3. **Capital Management Policy** – Capital targets, buffers, contingency plans
4. **Stress Testing Policy** – Scenario design, governance, use of results
5. **Model Risk Management Policy** – Model development, validation, governance
6. **Risk Data Aggregation and Reporting Policy** – Data quality, timeliness, completeness

#### 6.4.2 Escalation Framework

| Trigger Level | Action | Escalation To | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Green** (Within target) | Normal monitoring | Regular reporting | Standard |
| **Amber** (Approaching trigger) | Enhanced monitoring, contingency prep | CRO, Capital Committee | Within 5 business days |
| **Orange** (Trigger breached) | Activate contingency plan, management actions | CEO, Executive Committee | Within 2 business days |
| **Red** (Below regulatory minimum) | Emergency capital actions, regulator notification | Board, Regulator | Within 24 hours |

---

## 7. Data Architecture

### 7.1 Data Domains and Sources

| Data Domain | Source Systems | ICAAP Usage | Quality Requirements |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Counterparty Data** | Core Banking, CRM | Credit risk measurement, PD/LGD/EAD | Completeness > 99%, Timeliness T+1 |
| **Transaction Data** | Trading systems, Loan systems | Market risk, credit risk | Accuracy > 99.9%, Reconciliation daily |
| **Financial Data** | General Ledger, Budgeting | Capital resources, P&L projections | Audit trail, IFRS compliant |
| **Loss Data** | Operational risk system | Operational risk capital, scenarios | 7+ years history, unit-level detail |
| **Market Data** | Bloomberg, Reuters, EOD providers | Market risk, stress scenarios | Vendor validation, independent source |
| **Macroeconomic Data** | Central banks, statistical agencies | Scenario design, stress testing | Regular updates, forward-looking |
| **Regulatory Data** | Regulator publications | Benchmarking, regulatory ratios | Version controlled, timely updates |

### 7.2 Data Flow Architecture

```
+------------------+      +------------------+      +------------------+
| SOURCE SYSTEMS   | ---> | DATA INTEGRATION | ---> | RISK DATA WARE-  |
| - Core Banking   |      | LAYER            |      | HOUSE (RDW)      |
| - Trading        |      | - ETL/ELT        |      | - Central repos  |
| - ALM            |      | - Data quality    |      | - Historical     |
| - GL/Finance     |      | - Validation      |      | - Aggregated     |
| - HR/Operations  |      | - Lineage tracking|      | - Auditable      |
+------------------+      +------------------+      +------------------+
                                                           |
                                                           v
                              +------------------+      +------------------+
                              | ICAAP REPORTING  | <--- | CALCULATION      |
                              | & DASHBOARDS     |      | ENGINE           |
                              | - Capital ratios |      | - RWA calc       |
                              | - Stress results |      | - Stress models  |
                              | - Risk dashboards|      | - Capital proj   |
                              | - Reg. reporting |      | - Scenario sim   |
                              +------------------+      +------------------+
```

### 7.3 Data Quality Framework

| Quality Dimension | Control | Monitoring Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Reconciliation to source systems, tolerance thresholds | Daily |
| **Completeness** | Mandatory field validation, gap analysis | Daily |
| **Timeliness** | SLA-based data delivery monitoring | Intraday / Daily |
| **Consistency** | Cross-system validation, logic checks | Daily / Weekly |
| **Validity** | Reference data validation, format checks | Real-time |
| **Uniqueness** | Duplicate detection, entity resolution | Daily |
| **Integrity** | Referential integrity, lineage tracking | Weekly |

---

## 8. System Architecture and Technology Stack

### 8.1 Core Systems Map

| System | Function | Vendor/Platform | Integration Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core Banking System | Customer data, loan data | [Vendor] | API, Batch Files |
| Trading System | Market positions, P&L | [Vendor] | Real-time API, FIX |
| ALM System | IRRBB, Liquidity, FTP | [Vendor] | Database views, Web Services |
| Credit Risk System | PD/LGD/EAD models, Rating | [Vendor/In-house] | Batch, SOA |
| Market Risk System | VaR, Stress, Sensitivities | [Vendor] | Real-time, Batch |
| Operational Risk System | Loss data, RCSA, Scenarios | [Vendor] | Web Services, Batch |
| Financial Planning System | Budget, Forecast, Strategy | [Vendor] | Database, OLAP |
| Stress Testing Engine | Scenario generation, simulation | [In-house/Vendor] | Compute grid, DB |
| Risk Data Warehouse | Data aggregation, historization | [Vendor] | ETL/ELT, Data Lake |
| Reporting Platform | Dashboards, Regulatory reports | [Vendor] | BI tools, Web portal |

### 8.2 Technology Stack Requirements

| Layer | Technology Options | Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Database** | Oracle, SQL Server, PostgreSQL, Snowflake | ACID compliance, partitioning, versioning |
| **ETL/Data Integration** | Informatica, Talend, SSIS, Ab Initio | Data lineage, error handling, scheduling |
| **Calculation Engine** | SAS, MATLAB, Python/R, dedicated risk engines | Parallel processing, scenario simulation |
| **Reporting/Visualization** | Tableau, Power BI, Cognos, Qlik | Real-time drill-down, regulatory formats |
| **Workflow/Orchestration** | Apache Airflow, Control-M, Autosys | Dependency management, monitoring |
| **Data Governance** | Collibra, Informatica, Alation | Business glossary, lineage, stewardship |
| **Cloud Infrastructure** | AWS, Azure, GCP (if applicable) | Encryption, DR, region compliance |

---

## 9. ICAAP Documentation Architecture

### 9.1 Document Hierarchy

```
Level 0: ICAAP Policy (Strategic)
         |
Level 1: ICAAP Methodology Document (Framework)
         |
Level 2: Risk-specific Methodologies (Detailed)
  - Credit Risk ICAAP | Market Risk ICAAP | Operational Risk ICAAP
  - IRRBB ICAAP | Concentration Risk | Business Risk
         |
Level 3: Technical Documentation
  - Model Documentation | Data Dictionary | System Specifications
  - Validation Reports | Benchmarking Studies
         |
Level 4: Output Documents
  - ICAAP Report (Annual) | ICAAP Summary (Public Disclosure)
  - Stress Testing Report | Capital Plan | Risk Dashboard
```

### 9.2 ICAAP Report Structure

| Section | Content | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Executive Summary | Overall capital adequacy conclusion | All sections |
| Business Overview | Strategy, environment, changes | Strategic plan |
| Governance | Risk management framework, roles | Governance documentation |
| Risk Appetite | RAS, limits, triggers | RAF documentation |
| Risk Identification | Risk inventory, materiality assessment | Risk identification process |
| Risk Measurement | Methodologies by risk type | Risk-specific models |

| Stress Testing | Scenarios, results, management actions | Stress testing framework |
| Capital Planning | Targets, buffers, contingency plan | Capital planning process |

---

## 10. Integration with Recovery and Resolution Planning

### 10.1 Linkages

| Aspect | ICAAP Link | Recovery Plan Link | Resolution Plan Link |

| Capital Triggers | Early warning, target breaches | Recovery option triggers | Resolution entry points |
| Stress Scenarios | Adverse/Severely adverse | Recovery scenarios | Resolution scenarios |
| Management Actions | Capital restoration actions | Recovery options | Resolution measures |
| Communication | Internal escalation | Regulator notification | Resolution authorities |
| Time Horizon | 3-5 years | 12-24 months | Resolution execution |

### 10.2 Capital Continuum

```
+------------+  +------------+  +------------+  +------------+  +------------+
|  Normal    |  | Warning    |  | Recovery   |  | Resolution |  | Exit       |
| Operations |->| Zone       |->| Zone       |->| Zone       |->|            |
+------------+  +------------+  +------------+  +------------+  +------------+
     |               |               |               |               |
     | Target        | Trigger       | Recovery      | Resolution    |
     | Capital >     | Capital <     | Options       | Plan          |
     | 12.5% CET1    | 11.0% CET1    | Activated     | Executed      |
     +---------------+               |               |               |
                                     +---------------+               |
                                                     +---------------+
```

---

## 11. Control and Validation Framework

### 11.1 Independent Validation

| Validation Type | Frequency | Performed By | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model Validation | Annual / Material change | Model Validation Unit (independent) | All risk and capital models |
| Methodology Review | Annual | Internal Audit / External Consultant | ICAAP methodology, assumptions |
| Data Quality Audit | Quarterly | Data Governance Office | Critical data elements, lineage |
| System Access Review | Quarterly | IT Security | User access, segregation of duties |
| Process Walkthrough | Annual | Internal Audit | End-to-end ICAAP process |
| Peer Review | Biennial | Comparable institution / Industry body | Benchmarking of practices |

### 11.2 Key Controls

| Control ID | Control Description | Responsible Party | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| C-001 | Segregation of duties between risk-taking and risk oversight | CRO | Org chart, system access logs |
| C-002 | Model change control process | Model Governance | Change requests, approvals |
| C-003 | Data quality scorecards with threshold monitoring | Data Governance | Scorecard reports, issue logs |
| C-004 | Reconciliation of capital resources to financial statements | Finance | Reconciliation reports |
| C-005 | Independent review of stress test assumptions | Model Validation | Review sign-off documentation |
| C-006 | Board approval of ICAAP report and capital targets | Board Secretary | Board meeting minutes |

---

## 12. Implementation Roadmap

| Phase | Duration | Activities | Deliverables |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Foundation** | Months 1-3 | Governance setup, policy drafting, system selection | ICAAP Policy, Governance structure defined |
| **Phase 2: Build** | Months 4-8 | System implementation, data integration, model development | Technical architecture, Data flows established |
| **Phase 3: Integration** | Months 9-12 | Integration with planning cycle, stress testing framework, reporting | Integrated ICAAP-planning process, Stress test framework |
| **Phase 4: Validation** | Months 13-15 | Model validation, process testing, parallel run | Validation reports, Audit sign-off |
| **Phase 5: Go-Live** | Month 16 | Full implementation, regulatory submission | First full ICAAP report, Regulator submission |
| **Phase 6: Continuous Improvement** | Ongoing | BAU operation, enhancements, regulatory changes | Enhanced processes, Updated documentation |

name: Reverse Stress Test Framework - Non-Viability Conditions & Pathways
description: Comprehensive reverse stress test design document defining non-viability conditions, pathways, parameterization, and remediation actions for institution-wide stress testing.
content:
# INSTITUTION-WIDE REVERSE STRESS TEST FRAMEWORK

## 1. INTRODUCTION AND OBJECTIVES

Reverse stress testing identifies scenarios that could cause the institution to become non-viable, then works backward to determine what combination of risk factors could produce that outcome. Unlike forward stress tests (which apply shocks and measure impact), reverse stress tests start from the point of failure and identify the pathways that lead there.

### 1.1 Regulatory Context
- EBA Guidelines on Stress Testing (EBA/GL/2018/04)
- PRA SS6/14 - Stress testing and reverse stress testing
- Basel Committee guidelines for supervisory stress testing
- ICAAP/ILAAP regulatory requirements

### 1.2 Baseline Institution Profile (Assumed)
- CET1 Ratio: 13.0%
- Tier 1 Ratio: 14.5%
- Total Capital Ratio: 17.0%
- Leverage Ratio: 5.2%
- LCR: 140%
- NSFR: 115%
- RWA: EUR 200B
- Total Assets: EUR 600B
- Deposit Base: EUR 350B (60% retail, 40% wholesale)
- Loan Portfolio: EUR 400B (50% corporate, 25% retail mortgages, 15% SME, 10% other)
- Trading Book: EUR 80B

---

## 2. NON-VIABILITY CONDITIONS (POINT OF FAILURE)

### 2.1 Regulatory Capital Thresholds (Pillar 1)

Failure is defined as breaching ANY of the following regulatory minimums including combined buffer requirements:

| Metric | Minimum | With CCB (2.5%) | With CCyB (1%) | Total with all buffers |
|--------|---------|-----------------|----------------|----------------------|
| CET1 Ratio | 4.5% | 7.0% | 8.0% | 9.0%+ |
| Tier 1 Ratio | 6.0% | 8.5% | 9.5% | 10.5%+ |
| Total Capital | 8.0% | 10.5% | 11.5% | 12.5%+ |
| Leverage Ratio | 3.0% | 4.5% (if GSIB) | - | - |

Non-viability threshold (hard floor): CET1 < 4.5%, Tier 1 < 6%, Total Capital < 8%, or Leverage Ratio < 3%

### 2.2 Liquidity Thresholds (Pillar 2)

| Metric | Regulatory Minimum | Early Warning | Non-Viability |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| LCR | 100% | < 120% | < 100% for >= 5 consecutive days |
| NSFR | 100% | < 105% | < 100% sustained over quarter |
| Net stable funding gap | N/A | Negative trend | > 10% of total assets |

### 2.3 Franchise Viability Indicators (Qualitative Thresholds)

- Credit rating downgrade to sub-investment grade (BB+ or lower by S&P/Fitch, Ba1 by Moody's)
- Deposit run exceeding 15% of total deposits within 30 days
- Loss of access to unsecured wholesale funding markets
- CDS spreads exceeding 500bp sustained for > 10 trading days
- Material breach of regulatory waivers / covenant triggers on secured funding facilities
- Material loss of correspondent banking relationships

### 2.4 Composite Non-Viability Definition

The institution is deemed non-viable when any ONE of the following occurs:
(1) CET1 ratio falls below 4.5% AND cannot be restored within 30 days via capital actions
(2) LCR falls below 100% for 5+ consecutive days AND cannot be restored via liquidity actions
(3) The institution triggers a PONV (Point of Non-Viability) event as defined by the resolution authority
(4) A combination of Pillar 1 and Pillar 2 breaches that in aggregate threaten going-concern status

---

## 3. NON-VIABILITY PATHWAYS

### Pathway 1: CREDIT CRUNCH - Sovereign/Credit Shock

**Trigger:** Severe sovereign downgrade in home/host countries with contagion to corporate sector

**Propagation Mechanism:**
- Sovereign credit rating downgrade by 3-4 notches
- Government bond holdings lose 25-40% of market value -> AFS/HTM impairment
- Corporate default rates spike to 8-12% (vs baseline 2%)
- Loan loss provisions increase by 4-6x
- Risk weights increase due to rating migrations -> RWA inflation
- Collateral values decline 30-50% -> LGD increases

**Parameterization:**
```
PD_scalar = f(sovereign_downgrade_magnitude, sector_concentration)
PD_stressed = PD_baseline * PD_scalar * sector_multiplier

LGD_stressed = min(LGD_baseline + LGD_increment, 0.90)
where LGD_increment = 0.05 + 0.15 * (collateral_decline_pct / 0.40)

RWA_inflation = RWA_baseline * (1 + rating_migration_factor)
where rating_migration_factor ranges from 0.15 (mild) to 0.45 (severe)

Capital_impact = (ΔLLP + ΔMarket_Value_Losses + ΔRWA_inflation_charge) / Total_Capital
```

**Severity Calibration:**
| Severity | Sovereign Downgrade | Peak Default Rate | Loan Loss Rate | Capital Erosion |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Adverse | -2 notches | 6% | 2.5% | 3-4% of RWA |
| Severe | -3 notches | 9% | 4.0% | 6-8% of RWA |
| Extreme | -4+ notches | 12-15% | 6-8% | 10-12% of RWA |

**Time to Non-Viability (Extreme):** 6-9 months

---

### Pathway 2: MARKET DISLOCATION - Trading Book Blow-up

**Trigger:** Sudden market volatility event (similar to 2008 GFC, 2020 COVID, or 2022 LDI crisis)

**Propagation Mechanism:**
- Equity markets decline 40-60%
- Credit spreads widen dramatically (IG +300-500bp, HY +800-1500bp)
- Interest rate volatility spikes (VIX > 50, MOVE > 150)
- FX dislocations (EM currencies depreciate 20-40%)
- Counterparty credit risk (CVA/DVA volatility)
- Margin calls on derivatives and repo positions
- Forced asset sales at distressed prices -> downward spiral

**Parameterization:**
```
Trading_book_loss = f(risk_weighted_asset_class_exposures, shock_scenario)
Trading_book_loss = sum_over_exposures [ exposure_i * shock_i * correlation_factor ]

where:
- Equity shock: 0.40 to 0.60
- IG credit spread widening: 300-500bp
- HY credit spread widening: 800-1500bp
- FX EM depreciation: 20-40%
- Interest rate shock: parallel shift +200-300bp, steepening +50-100bp

Correlation_factor = 0.7 (adverse), 0.85 (severe), 1.0 (extreme - stress correlations)

Margin_call_size = (VM_initial - VM_marked) + IM_additional
where IM_additional can be 2-5x initial IM during stress
```

**Severity Calibration:**
| Severity | Equity Decline | IG Spread Widening | Trading Loss | Capital Impact |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Adverse | -25% | +200bp | 5-8% of trading book | 2-3% |
| Severe | -40% | +400bp | 12-18% of trading book | 5-8% |
| Extreme | -55% | +800bp | 25-35% with feedback effects | 10-15%+ |

**Time to Non-Viability (Extreme):** 2-7 days (can be hours in severe liquidity dry-up)

---

### Pathway 3: LIQUIDITY CRISIS - Wholesale Funding Freeze

**Trigger:** Loss of confidence leading to wholesale funding withdrawal and/or deposit run

**Propagation Mechanism:**
- Credit rating downgrade triggers wholesale funding withdrawal
- Unsecured debt cannot be rolled over (CP, MTN, unsecured bonds)
- Secured funding haircuts increase (repo, covered bonds)
- Retail deposit run accelerates (digital banking amplifies speed)
- Central bank facilities become sole funding source
- Asset fire sales at distressed prices -> capital erosion

**Parameterization:**
```
LCR_stressed = (HQLA - haircut_adjustment - encumbered_assets) / (Net_cash_outflows * stress_multiplier)

Net_cash_outflows_stressed = Net_cash_outflows_baseline * (1 + outflow_acceleration)
where outflow_acceleration = 0.3 (adverse), 0.7 (severe), 1.5 (extreme)

Wholesale_rollover_rate = base_rate * (1 - downgrade_impact)
where downgrade_impact = 0.3 (1-notch), 0.6 (2-notch), 0.9 (3-notch or to HY)

Deposit_run_rate = daily_withdrawal_rate * (1 + rumor_multiplier)
where rumor_multiplier = 2x (adverse), 5x (severe), 10x+ (extreme)
```

**Severity Calibration:**
| Severity | Funding Gap | Deposit Run | LCR Nadir | Survival Horizon |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-----------|------------------|
| Adverse | 15% | 5% | 105% | 60 days |
| Severe | 35% | 10% | 85% | 21 days |
| Extreme | 60%+ | 25%+ | 55% | < 7 days |

**Time to Non-Viability (Extreme):** 3-14 days

---

### Pathway 4: OPERATIONAL CATASTROPHE - Cyber/Systemic Failure

**Trigger:** Major cyber attack, system failure, or operational risk event causing financial and reputational damage

**Propagation Mechanism:**
- Core banking system compromise -> transaction processing halted
- Data breach -> regulatory fines, litigation, remediation costs
- Reputational damage -> customer attrition, deposit outflows
- Business disruption -> revenue loss, compensation costs
- Regulatory sanctions -> capital add-ons, business restrictions

**Parameterization:**
```
Operational_loss = direct_loss + indirect_loss + reputational_loss

direct_loss = system_recovery_cost + fraud_losses + regulatory_fines
indirect_loss = revenue_disruption * duration_days + customer_compensation
reputational_loss = franchise_value_loss + (deposit_outflow_percentage * deposit_base)

Capital_impact = (Operational_loss - insurance_recovery) / CET1_Capital

Recovery_time_factor = f(system_resilience, incident_response_capability)
```

**Severity Calibration:**
| Severity | System Downtime | Direct Loss | Reputational Damage | Capital Impact |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Adverse | 1-3 days | EUR 500M-1B | Deposit outflow 3% | 0.5-1.0% |
| Severe | 1-2 weeks | EUR 2-5B | Deposit outflow 8% | 2.0-4.0% |
| Extreme | 3-4 weeks+ | EUR 8-15B+ | Deposit outflow 20%+ | 5.0-10.0%+ |

**Time to Non-Viability (Extreme):** 1-4 weeks (could be faster if combined with liquidity run)

---

### Pathway 5: MACROECONOMIC DEPRESSION - Protracted Recession

**Trigger:** Prolonged deep recession with deflationary spiral or stagflation

**Propagation Mechanism:**
- GDP contraction of 8-15% over 2-3 years
- Unemployment rate peaks at 15-20%
- Prolonged low/negative interest rates -> net interest margin compression
- Commercial real estate values decline 40-60%
- Consumer loan defaults (credit cards, auto, personal loans) spike
- Cumulative loan losses across all portfolios over multiple years
- Negative carry on structural hedges

**Parameterization:**
```
GDP_shock = annual_contraction_rate * duration_years
gdp_impact_range: -0.04 to -0.06 (adverse), -0.06 to -0.10 (severe), -0.10 to -0.15 (extreme)

Cumulative_loan_loss_rate = f(GDP_shock, unemployment_peak, CRE_decline)
Cumulative_loan_loss_rate = -0.015 - 0.25*GDP_shock + 0.10*(unemployment_peak - 0.05) + 0.08*CRE_decline

NIM_compression = baseline_NIM * (1 - NIM_stress_factor)
where NIM_stress_factor = 0.15 (adverse), 0.30 (severe), 0.50 (extreme)

Multi_year_P&L = sum_over_years [ (NII_stressed - LLP - opex) * discount_factor ]
```

**Severity Calibration:**
| Severity | GDP Peak-to-Trough | Unemployment Peak | Cumulative Loss Rate | Final CET1 |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|------------|
| Adverse | -4% | 9% | 4-5% | 9-10% |
| Severe | -8% | 14% | 8-10% | 5-7% |
| Extreme | -15% | 20%+ | 14-18% | < 4.5% |

**Time to Non-Viability (Extreme):** 18-36 months (slow burn, prolonged erosion)

---

## 4. CROSS-PATHWAY CORRELATION AND CONCENTRATION ANALYSIS

### 4.1 Correlation Matrix of Pathways

| Pathway | Credit | Market | Liquidity | Operational | Macro |
|---------|--------|--------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Credit | 1.0 | 0.6 | 0.7 | 0.1 | 0.8 |
| Market | 0.6 | 1.0 | 0.8 | 0.2 | 0.5 |
| Liquidity | 0.7 | 0.8 | 1.0 | 0.3 | 0.6 |
| Operational | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.3 | 1.0 | 0.1 |
| Macro | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.1 | 1.0 |

### 4.2 Concentration Risk Triggers

- Single-name concentration: Any single counterparty exposure > 10% of CET1
- Sector concentration: Any sector > 25% of total loan book
- Geographic concentration: Any country > 30% of total exposure
- Funding concentration: Any single funding source > 15% of total funding
- Collateral concentration: Any single collateral type > 40% of secured funding pool

---

## 5. REMEDIATION ACTION LINKAGES

### 5.1 Stage-Based Remediation Framework

Each pathway has distinct actions triggered by specific thresholds:

| Stage | Trigger | Capital Actions | Liquidity Actions | Operational Actions |
|-------|---------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Stage 1: Early Warning** | CET1 < 10.5% or LCR < 130% | Reduce dividends, limit share buybacks, optimize RWA | Increase tenor of wholesale funding, build HQLA buffer | Activate enhanced monitoring, stress testing committee convened |
| **Stage 2: Capital Erosion** | CET1 < 8.5% or LCR < 115% or 1-notch downgrade | Reduce discretionary lending, asset sales (non-core), cancel dividends | Draw committed credit lines, increase collateral pool, reduce wholesale funding reliance | Risk limits tightened, contingency funding plan activated |
| **Stage 3: Severe Stress** | CET1 < 6.5% or LCR < 105% or 2-notch downgrade | Capital raise (rights issue/AT1), liability management, portfolio hedging | Access central bank facilities, emergency asset securitization, reduce secured funding haircuts | Business continuity plan, management restructuring |
| **Stage 4: Breach/Non-Viability** | CET1 < 4.5% or LCR < 100% / 5+ days or PONV triggered | Regulatory capital instruments conversion/write-down, bail-in execution | Emergency liquidity support, resolution funding arrangements | Recovery plan execution, engage resolution authority, prepare for resolution |

### 5.2 Pathway-Specific Remediation Actions

**Pathway 1 (Credit Crunch):**
- Early: Increase credit risk monitoring, tighten underwriting standards, sell/hedge concentrated exposures
- Erosion: Reduce corporate lending, increase provisions, sell non-strategic loan portfolios
- Severe: Capital raise, government/private credit support, asset protection scheme
- Breach: Bad bank transfer, loan portfolio sale, capital injection

**Pathway 2 (Market Dislocation):**
- Early: Reduce VaR limits, increase hedging, reduce leveraged positions
- Erosion: De-risk trading book, close proprietary positions, reduce derivative notional
- Severe: Wind down trading operations, sell liquid positions immediately, access central bank market operations
- Breach: Full trading book wind-down, portfolio transfer to resolution vehicle

**Pathway 3 (Liquidity Crisis):**
- Early: Extend funding maturities, maintain committed lines, diversify funding sources
- Erosion: Activate contingency funding plan, draw committed facilities, increase collateral posting
- Severe: Access central bank standing facilities (MLA, etc.), emergency bond/loan securitization, public sector liquidity support
- Breach: Emergency liquidity assistance from central bank, resolution liquidity facility

**Pathway 4 (Operational Catastrophe):**
- Early: IT resiliency testing, cyber insurance review, incident response drills
- Erosion: Activate disaster recovery, contain breach, maintain manual processing capability
- Severe: Engage regulators, crisis communication, customer restitution plan
- Breach: System rebuild, strategic outsourcing, business continuity wind-down

**Pathway 5 (Macroeconomic Depression):**
- Early: Cost cutting, branch optimization, loan restructuring capabilities
- Erosion: Freeze hiring/salary, reduce discretionary spending, increase collection capacity
- Severe: Strategic divestitures, rights issue, debt-for-equity swaps
- Breach: Government intervention, nationalization, orderly wind-down

### 5.3 Remediation Capacity Assessment
```
Total_Remediation_Capacity = Capital_Raise_Capacity + Asset_Sale_Proceeds + Contingent_Liquidity + Insurance_Recovery

Capital_Raise_Capacity = min(equity_market_cap * issuance_capacity_pct, max_institutional_appetite)
where issuance_capacity_pct = 0.10 to 0.30 (depending on market conditions)

Asset_Sale_Proceeds = sum_over_assets [ asset_value * (1 - fire_sale_discount) ]
where fire_sale_discount = 0.05 (liquid assets) to 0.50 (illiquid assets)

Contingent_Liquidity = Committed_lines_unused + Central_bank_facilities + Secured_funding_capacity
```

---

## 6. SCENARIO QUANTIFICATION FRAMEWORK

### 6.1 Single-Factor Reverse Stress Test (Simplest)

"What single shock would cause CET1 < 4.5%?"

Given baseline CET1 = 13.0% and RWA = EUR 200B:
Total capital erosion needed = (0.13 - 0.045) * 200B = EUR 17B

Answer: A credit loss of EUR 17B (4.25% of total loan portfolio), OR a trading loss of EUR 17B (21% of trading book), OR a combination as defined by:
```
alpha * (credit_loss / credit_loss_threshold) + beta * (market_loss / market_loss_threshold) >= 1
where alpha + beta = 1 (convex combination)
```

### 6.2 Multi-Factor Reverse Stress Test (Standard)

Find combinations of risk factors that produce the non-viability condition:
```
minimize ||theta|| subject to: f(theta) >= viability_threshold

where:
theta = vector of risk factor shocks (macro, credit, market, liquidity)
f(theta) = loss function mapping shocks to capital/liquidity impact
viability_threshold = CET1 < 4.5% OR LCR < 100% OR PONV trigger
```

### 6.3 Stress Horizon and Path Dependency

| Pathway | Horizon | Granularity | Path Dependency |
|---------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| Credit Crunch | 9 quarters | Quarterly | Yes - credit migration path matters |
| Market Dislocation | 2 weeks | Daily | Low - mostly instantaneous |
| Liquidity Crisis | 1 quarter | Daily | Very high - sequential runs matter |
| Operational Catastrophe | 1 month | Weekly | Medium - response effectiveness |
| Macro Depression | 12 quarters | Quarterly | Very high - cumulative effects |

---

## 7. GOVERNANCE AND MONITORING

### 7.1 Frequency of Reverse Stress Testing
- Full reverse stress test: Annually (board-approved)
- Intermediate update: Semi-annually
- Ad-hoc trigger: Any material change in risk profile, business model, or external environment
- Rapid reverse stress test: Quarterly (simplified, single-factor)

### 7.2 Governance Structure
- Board Risk Committee: Approve methodology, review results, approve remediation actions
- CRO / Risk Management: Design and execute tests, report results
- ALCO / Treasury: Provide liquidity assumptions, review funding pathways
- Business Heads: Validate business-specific assumptions and recovery actions
- Internal Audit: Independent review of framework and execution

### 7.3 Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) for Monitoring

| KRI | Threshold | Frequency | Trigger Pathway |
|-----|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| CDS Spread | > 200bp (warning), > 500bp (severe) | Daily | All |
| Loan Delinquency Rate | > 2x baseline (warning), > 4x (severe) | Monthly | Credit, Macro |
| Wholesale Funding Spread | > 50bp (warning), > 150bp (severe) | Daily | Liquidity |
| Deposit Outflow Rate | > 0.5%/week (warning), > 2%/week (severe) | Weekly | Liquidity, OpRisk |
| VaR Utilization | > 70% (warning), > 90% (severe) | Daily | Market |
| RWA Density | > 55% (warning), > 65% (severe) | Monthly | Credit |
| Cyber Incident Severity | Level 2+ (warning), Level 4+ (severe) | Real-time | Operational |
| Regulatory Capital Ratio | Buffer erosion > 30% (warning), > 60% (severe) | Weekly | All |

### 7.4 Reverse Stress Test Output Metrics

For each pathway, the output must include:
1. Point of non-viability (exact breach trigger)
2. Time horizon to non-viability
3. Risk factors and shock magnitudes required
4. Probability assessment (even if qualitative)
5. Key assumptions and limitations
6. Remediation actions mapped to each stage
7. Remediation capacity assessment
8. Residual non-viability risk after remediation

---

## 8. WORKED EXAMPLE: CREDIT PATHWAY REVERSE STRESS TEST

**Step 1: Define non-viability**
CET1 falls below 4.5% (from 13.0% baseline)
Required capital erosion: EUR 17B

**Step 2: Identify minimal shock combination**
We seek the combination of credit losses, market losses, and RWA inflation that produces EUR 17B erosion:

Let:
- L = credit losses (EUR B)
- M = market losses (trading/AVA, EUR B)
- R = RWA inflation impact (higher capital charge from increased RWAs)

Constraint: L + M + R >= 17B

Using optimization to find the 'most plausible' combination:

Scenario A (Credit-centric): L = 12B, M = 2B, R = 3B (RWA increase from EUR 200B to EUR 230B)
- PD increase: from 2% to 8% average
- LGD increase: from 40% to 55%
- Loss rate: 8% * 55% = 4.4% on EUR 400B loans = EUR 17.6B (with concentration)
- RWA inflation: downgrade migration increases risk weights by 15%

Scenario B (Market-centric): L = 5B, M = 8B, R = 4B
- Trading book blow-up: 10% loss on EUR 80B
- Credit losses from correlated defaults

**Step 3: Assess plausibility**
Scenario A: PD of 8% is approximately 2008 GFC level for corporate loans - plausible but severe
Scenario B: Trading loss of 10% is possible in a COVID-style shock but alone insufficient

**Step 4: Remediation mapping**
At CET1 hitting 8.5% (Stage 2): Reduce lending by 20%, sell EUR 5B non-core assets, suspend dividends
At CET1 hitting 6.5% (Stage 3): Rights issue EUR 5B, sell EUR 10B loan portfolio, access govt schemes
At CET1 < 4.5% (Stage 4): Bail-in execution, resolution proceedings

**Step 5: Residual risk assessment**
After full remediation capacity (EUR 12B capital raise + EUR 5B asset sales), remaining CET1 = 13% - (17-12)/200 = 13% - 2.5% = 10.5% - but this assumes remediation is successful in time, which is highly uncertain during stress.

---

## 9. LIMITATIONS AND MODEL RISK

1. **Path dependency**: Reverse stress tests are inherently sensitive to the sequence of events; single-point estimates may miss non-linear dynamics
2. **Correlation breakdown**: In stress, historical correlations break down; our extreme scenarios use 1.0 correlation which is conservative
3. **Behavioral feedback**: Management actions, market reactions, and customer behavior interact in ways that are difficult to model
4. **Time horizon mismatch**: Some pathways (macro) take years while others (market) take days - comparing across pathways requires careful judgment
5. **Model risk**: The quantitative models are simplifications and may miss tail dependencies or second-order effects
6. **Remediation feasibility**: Assumed remediation capacity may not be available during systemic stress when all banks need capital simultaneously

---

## 10. CONCLUSIONS

The reverse stress test framework identifies five distinct pathways to non-viability, with the most probable being a combination of credit and liquidity stresses (Pathways 1 + 3) or a severe market dislocation (Pathway 2). The institution's primary vulnerabilities are:

1. **High correlation between credit and liquidity pathways** - the most dangerous scenario
2. **Concentration in corporate lending** - sensitive to sector-specific shocks
3. **Wholesale funding reliance** - exposed to market confidence shifts
4. **Operational risk amplification** - cyber events can trigger liquidity crises

The framework provides a structured, repeatable process for identifying the point of non-viability, the pathways that lead there, and the remediation actions that can prevent or mitigate failure. This is an essential input to the ICAAP, ILAAP, and Recovery Plan.

## BASELINE INSTITUTION PROFILE

## PATHWAY PARAMETERIZATION SUMMARY

| Pathway | Primary Risk Driver | Key Parameterization Formula | Non-Viability Horizon |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1. Credit Crunch | PD, LGD, RWA inflation | PD_stressed = PD_baseline * PD_scalar * sector_multiplier | 6-9 months |
| 2. Market Dislocation | Equity, credit spread, FX shocks | Trading_loss = sum(exposure_i * shock_i * corr_factor) | 2-7 days |
| 3. Liquidity Crisis | Funding gap, deposit run, haircuts | LCR_stressed = HQLA_adj / (Outflows * stress_multiplier) | 3-14 days |
| 4. Operational Catastrophe | Direct + indirect + reputational loss | Op_loss = recovery_cost + fraud + fines + franchise_loss | 1-4 weeks |
| 5. Macro Depression | GDP, unemployment, CRE decline | Cum_loss = -0.015 - 0.25*GDP + 0.10*Unemp + 0.08*CRE | 18-36 months |

## SEVERITY CALIBRATION - CAPITAL EROSION (CET1 impact in percentage points)

| Securitization | 3-8 | 3-6 months | Medium |
| Central bank facilities | 5-20+ | Immediate-Days | Low (but stigma) |
| Liability management | 2-5 | 4-8 weeks | Medium |

Total Remediation Capacity: EUR 25-80B depending on market conditions

## CROSS-PATHWAY CORRELATION

# REVERSE STRESS TEST - REMEDIATION ACTION MATRIX

## Stage Definitions

- **Stage 1 (Early Warning):** CET1 < 10.5% or LCR < 130% or KRI breaches
- **Stage 2 (Capital Erosion):** CET1 < 8.5% or LCR < 115% or 1-notch rating downgrade
- **Stage 3 (Severe Stress):** CET1 < 6.5% or LCR < 105% or 2-notch downgrade
- **Stage 4 (Breach/Non-Viability):** CET1 < 4.5% or LCR < 100% sustained or PONV trigger

---

## PATHWAY 1: CREDIT CRUNCH - Remediation Actions

| Stage | Action | Capacity | Timeline | Effectiveness |
|-------|--------|----------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | Increase credit monitoring | N/A | Immediate | Medium |
| 1 | Tighten underwriting standards | N/A | Immediate | Medium |
| 1 | Reduce single-name concentration | Low | 1-3 months | High |
| 1 | Hedge credit exposures (CDS) | Medium | 1-4 weeks | Medium |
| 2 | Reduce discretionary lending | EUR 2-5B cap relief | 1-3 months | Medium |
| 2 | Sell non-core loan portfolios | EUR 3-8B | 3-6 months | High |
| 2 | Increase provisions | Cost (EUR 1-3B) | Immediate | Low (loss recognition) |
| 2 | Cancel dividends | EUR 1-2B | Immediate | High |
| 3 | Capital raise (rights issue) | EUR 3-8B | 6-12 weeks | High |
| 3 | AT1 coupon cancellation | EUR 0.5-1.5B/yr | Immediate | Medium |
| 3 | Portfolio hedging/insurance | EUR 2-5B protection | 2-6 weeks | Medium |
| 3 | Government guarantee schemes | Up to EUR 10B | 4-8 weeks | High (if available) |
| 4 | Bail-in execution | EUR 5-15B | Immediate | High (but destructive) |

| 4 | Resolution authority engagement | N/A | Immediate | Critical |

## PATHWAY 2: MARKET DISLOCATION - Remediation Actions

|-------|--------|----------|----------|--------------|

| 2 | Reduce discretionary spending | EUR 300-800M/yr | 1-3 months | Medium |
| 2 | Increase collection workforce | EUR 50-100M | 1-3 months | High |
| 2 | Loan modification programs | N/A | 1-3 months | Medium |

| 3 | Rights issue | EUR 3-8B | 6-12 weeks | High |
| 3 | Debt-for-equity swaps | EUR 2-5B | 3-6 months | Medium (dilutive) |
| 3 | Asset sales (non-core + core) | EUR 10-20B | 6-12 months | High |
| 4 | Government intervention / bailout | EUR 10-50B | 1-3 months | High (conditional) |
| 4 | Nationalization | N/A | Immediate | Final resort |
| 4 | Orderly wind-down / resolution | N/A | 3-12 months | Last resort |

## QUANTITATIVE REMEDIATION CAPACITY SUMMARY

| Remediation Source | Capacity Range (EUR B) | Reliability | Speed |
|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| Capital actions (dividends, AT1, rights) | 5-17 | Medium-High | Medium |
| Asset sales (non-core, liquid) | 10-25 | Medium | Medium-Fast |
| Central bank facilities | 15-70 | High | Fast |
| Loan portfolio sales/securitization | 8-23 | Low-Medium | Slow |
| Insurance/recoveries | 1-3 | Low-Medium | Slow |
| Government support | 10-50 | Low (non-systemic) | Medium |
| Operational cost savings | 1-2 | High | Slow |
| **Total** | **25-80** | **Varies** | **Dependent** |

## RESIDUAL RISK ASSESSMENT

After applying all feasible remediation actions, the residual non-viability risk is assessed for each pathway:

| Pathway | Post-Remediation CET1 (Best Case) | Post-Remediation CET1 (Worst Case) | Residual Risk |
|---------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------|
| Credit Crunch | 9.5% | 4.0% (breach) | Medium |
| Market Dislocation | 10.0% | 3.5% (breach) | Low-Medium |
| Liquidity Crisis | 8.0% | 2.0% (breach) | Medium-High |
| Operational Catastrophe | 11.0% | 6.0% | Low |
| Macro Depression | 7.5% | 2.5% (breach) | High |

The macro depression pathway carries the highest residual risk due to the prolonged nature and limited remediation capacity during systemic stress when all banks compete for the same capital and liquidity.
